role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3011 Advertising Sales Agents 8.2% + 41-3091 Sales Reps of Services 3.0% of wage bill — record's role_mix is 4-digit 5418 (agency-dominated proxy); in the hypothesized dominant sub-segment (promotional-products distribution) sales/account staff are the single largest role, est. well above the proxy shares
role_hints.note: Product search, quoting, virtual mockups and order paperwork automate heavily (AI already does supplier search and artwork proofs); the client relationship — corporate buyers reordering branded merch — is the distributor's whole moat and persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-9011 Demonstrators and Product Promoters 1.6% (5418 proxy — understated for the demo/sampling and event-marketing firms inside this catch-all)
role_hints.note: In-store demos, sampling and event staffing are physical presence work; scheduling automates, performance does not.
Logo placement, imprint proofs, spec sheets and virtuals are near-fully AI-automatable — suppliers and platforms already generate them.

role_hints.oews_basis: 27-3031 Public Relations Specialists 8.3% + 13-1161 Market Research Analysts 6.6% (5418 proxy — largely agency contamination; thinner in real 541890 firms)
role_hints.note: Campaign reporting, program recaps and merch-store management automate substantially.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General & Operations Managers 9.3% + 11-2021 Marketing Managers 5.2% (5418 proxy)
role_hints.note: Supplier negotiation and key-account stewardship persist; ops reporting and inventory/program admin automate.

role_hints.role: Order operations, fulfillment coordination and admin
role_hints.oews_basis: long tail of office/admin and shipping/receiving occupations not in the listed top-10, est. remainder ~25-35%
role_hints.note: PO entry, supplier chasing, proof routing and billing automate near-fully; physical kitting/fulfillment where the firm holds inventory does not.

source_hints.source: ASI (Advertising Specialty Institute) Counselor State of the Industry report
source_hints.why: The promo-products trade data standard — distributor revenue, tech/AI adoption and margin benchmarks for the hypothesized dominant sub-segment.

source_hints.source: PPAI (Promotional Products Association International) research and market-size estimates
source_hints.why: Second primary association; cross-check on segment size and distributor counts vs. the catch-all NAICS total.
The largest direct/online promo distributor publishes real margins and marketing economics — the benchmark for what platform competition does to distributor pricing.

source_hints.source: HALO Branded Solutions / iPROMOTEu / American Solutions for Business acquisition announcements and PE sponsor disclosures
source_hints.why: Documented distributor roll-up activity — consolidator counts for B; deal terms occasionally disclosed for M.
Distributor principals skew older and firms are micro (4,615 of 6,397 firms <5 employees); ASI surveys carry owner-age and exit-intent data.
For the demo/sampling/field-marketing sub-segment: a public consolidator whose filings show segment margins and acquisition history.

capture_questions: Promo distributors price as a markup over supplier cost (industry-standard ~30-40% gross margins) — when AI automates sourcing, virtuals and order admin, does the distributor keep the saving as margin, or do online platforms (4imprint, custom-merch sites) with AI-native self-service compress the markup toward commodity resale?

Who owns the client relationship — the distributorship, or the individual salesperson who can move a book of corporate accounts to another distributor (the industry runs on affiliate/rep models with portable books)? What does a roll-up actually acquire?

What stops a corporate buyer from using generic AI to design merch and order directly from suppliers or print-on-demand platforms, bypassing the distributor entirely — is supplier access (ASI/SAGE membership gates) still a real moat?

For the demo/event sub-segment: staffing is the product and clients pay per event-day — is there any AI capture at all beyond thin scheduling/admin savings, and does that sub-segment dilute the code's average?
terminal_value_question: For the dominant promo-products sub-segment the product is physical branded merchandise — AI cannot print a mug — so mature AI attacks the distributor's intermediation (search, artwork, ordering) rather than the product; the deciding mechanism is whether supplier-access gates and corporate procurement inertia keep the distributor in the flow, or whether AI-native direct platforms (4imprint's model, print-on-demand) make the independent distributor an unnecessary hop. Is this operator a relationship-protected reseller or a disintermediation casualty?
special_notes: Catch-all code: 541890 spans promotional-products distribution, in-store demonstration/sampling services, event/experiential marketing support, display lettering, aerial advertising and media-adjacent services. Identify the top-3 revenue sub-segments, score the DOMINANT one, and set heterogeneous: true. Hypothesis (flagged as such): promotional-products distribution is the dominant sub-segment by firm count and revenue — verify against ASI/PPAI market-size estimates (~$25B+ industry) vs. the code's $19.4B SUSB receipts.

Role-mix contamination: the 4-digit 5418 proxy imports ad-agency occupations (PR specialists 8.3%, market research 6.6%) that are marginal here. Re-weight toward sales, order operations and field staff before setting within-role fractions.

EBITDA normalization: distributor P&Ls often run on gross-margin dollars with commission-only sales reps (50%+ of gross to the rep); owner comp sits inside SDE at the micro end; affiliate-network firms (iPROMOTEu model) book gross vs. net revenue inconsistently — normalize revenue recognition before comparing margins or multiples.
Key-person portability: sales reps own account relationships and routinely move books between distributorships — the industry's roll-up risk is buying revenue that walks. Weight pi_moat accordingly.

Boundary confusion: ad agencies 541810, media reps 541840, display advertising 541850, direct mail 541860, material distribution 541870, sign manufacturing 339950, merch screen printing 323113 — promo 'industry' figures from ASI/PPAI include supplier-side firms (manufacturers/imprinters) that are NOT in 541890; strip suppliers before mapping to this code.
Consolidation activity (HALO, BAMKO, Advantage in demos) is usable for B and M only — never as evidence for V or A (rule 2).
